title: Real Gases and Van der Waal's Equation

concepts.title: Derivation of real gases from ideal behavior
concepts.conceptBody:
Deviation from Ideal Behaviour (Real Gases)

If measurements of pressure, volume and temperature of a gas do not confirm to the ideal gas law ($\text{PV}=\text{RT}$ ), within the precision of measurements, the gas is said to deviate from ideal behavior and exhibit non-ideal behaviour. Such gases are called real gases. At low pressures and moderately high temperatures, real gases obey ideal gas equation but as the pressure is increased or the temperature is decreased, a marked deviation from ideal behaviour is observed.

To display the deviation more clearly, the ratio of the observed molar volume $\left( {{\text{V}}_{\text{m}}} \right)$ to the ideal molar volume $\left( {{\text{V}}_{\text{m}}} \right.$, ideal $\left. =\frac{\text{RT}}{\text{P}} \right)$ is plotted as a function of pressure at constant temperature. This ratio is called the compressibility factor $(\text{Z})$, which is expressed as

$\text{Z}=\frac{{{\text{V}}_{\text{m}}}}{{{\text{V}}_{\text{m, ideal }}}}=\frac{\text{P}\times {{\text{V}}_{\text{m}}}}{\text{RT}}=\frac{\text{PV}}{\text{nRT}}$

For an ideal gas $\text{Z}=1$ and is independent of pressure and temperature. For a real gas, $\text{Z}=\text{f}$ $(\text{T},\text{P})$, a function of both temperature and pressure.

Above plot of $\text{Z}$ as a function of pressure at varying temperature implies that curve for a real gas has a tendency to coincide with that of an ideal gas at high temperature, i.e., at extremely high temperature; the behaviour of a real gas approaches that of an ideal gas.

For hydrogen, $\text{Z}$ is greater than unity (ideal value) at all pressures. For $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$ and $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}},\text{Z}$ is less than unity in the lower part of the pressure ranges but is greater than unity at very high pressures. For gases, which are easily liquefied, Z dip sharply below the ideal line, i.e., Z decreases with increase of pressure in the lower pressure range, passes through a minimum at some pressure and then increases continuously with pressure in the higher pressure region.

Reasons for deviations:

In order to explain the deviations from ideal behaviour, it is necessary to have a look at the kinetic theory of gases. The following two postulates of kinetic theory do not hold good under all conditions.

Postulate no. 1: The volume occupied by the molecules themselves is negligibly small as compared to the total volume occupied by the gas. This can be justified when pressure is low or temperature is high. But, if the pressure becomes too high or the temperature is too low, the volume occupied by the molecules will no longer be negligible.

Postulate no. 2: The forces of attraction between gas molecules are negligible. Again this postulate is not valid at high pressures and low temperatures.

concepts.title: Correction for force of attraction
concepts.conceptBody:
Correction for Forces of attraction:

Consider molecules of layer A \& B. As can be seen in Fig., molecules of layer A, which are closest to the container wall strike the wall of the container and theory exert a pressure $\text{P}$ on it. This is the pressure exerted by the molecules of the eral gas. The molecules of layer B attract the molecules of layer A but do not hit the container wall. This backward pull experienced by the molecules of layer A causes them to exert less pressures than what they would have exerted if there were no attractive forces.

Therefore, if a real gas exerts pressure $\text{P}$, then an ideal gas would exert pressure $\text{P}+\text{p}$ where $p$ is the pressure lost by the real gas due to attractive forces.

The attractive force is mutual. That is to say that this force is between layer A and layer B molecules. Since force between two mutually attracting objects is directly proportional to the concentration of layer A and layer B molecules, since force between two mutually attracting objective is directly proportion to the magnitude of the cause of force, the pressure $\text{P}$ lost by real gases proportion to the concentration of layer A and B molecules.

$\text{p}\propto \frac{\text{n}}{\text{V}}(\text{ layer A})$

$\text{p}\propto \frac{\text{n}}{\text{V}}(\text{ layer B})$

$\text{p}\propto \frac{{{\text{n}}^{2}}}{~{{\text{V}}^{2}}}\text{p}\propto \frac{\text{a}{{\text{n}}^{2}}}{~{{\text{V}}^{2}}}$

where $\text{a}$ is the proportionality constant and is a measure of the forces of attraction between the molecules. Thus, ${{\text{P}}_{\text{ideal }}}={{\text{P}}_{\text{real }}}+\frac{\text{a}{{\text{n}}^{2}}}{~{{\text{V}}^{2}}}$

The unit of the term $\frac{\text{a}{{\text{n}}^{2}}}{~{{\text{V}}^{2}}}$ will be the same as that of the pressure. Thus, the SI units of a will be $\text{Pa}{{\text{m}}^{6}}~\text{mo}{{\text{l}}^{-2}}$. It may be conveniently expressed in $\text{kPad}{{\text{m}}^{6}}~\text{mo}{{\text{l}}^{-2}}$.

Now, the real gas behaves same as ideal gas on removing the force of attraction. The only difference that now remains is the molecular size.

concepts.title: Correction for Molecular sizes
concepts.conceptBody:
Correction for molecular size

The container of volume $\text{V}$ has $\text{n}$ mole of gas molecules each of which possess a certain volume of their own. If the total volume blocked by 1 mole of molecules due to their sheer presence is taken as $b$, then the total volume blocked by them would be nb. We can now look at the container and see that the effective volume for the gas molecules to move is $\text{V}-\text{nb}$. This means that in the volume, $\text{V}-\text{nb}$ the gas molecules have zero volume of their own.

Since, ${{P}_{i}}{{V}_{i}}=nRT$

$\left( \text{P}+\frac{\text{a}{{\text{n}}^{2}}}{~{{\text{V}}^{2}}} \right)(\text{V}-\text{nb})=\text{nRT}\quad {{\text{V}}_{\text{real }}}={{\text{V}}_{\text{ideal }}}-\text{nb}$

‘$\text{b}$’ is called the excluded volume or co-volume. The numerical value of $\text{b}$ is four times the actual volume occupied by 1 mole of gas molecules. This can be shown as follows.

We assure that molecules are in constant motion and collide with other molecules at every instant. Moreover bi-molecular collisions are much more frequent than tri-molecular etc. Since all molecules are alike we shall assume, for the sake of simplicity (without introducing error) that molecules have fixed ‘partners’ for collision. That is molecule $1\text{ }\!\!\And\!\!\text{ }2,3\text{ }\!\!\And\!\!\text{ }4$ etc. If the total molecules are odd we can remove one molecule (removal of one molecule would hardly make a difference) to make it even. But since we have already considered that molecules collide every instant, fixing partners for collisions would introduce an error. This is because after one collision they would need to separate in order to collide again and since we have fixed partners they cannot collide with other molecules. This leads to a time interval, which is collision free. To take care of this, we now assume that molecules are stuck to each other in pairs as shown in Fig. This creates another problem that when the molecules collide they collide in all possible directions.

To simulate this we assume that the molecules that are stuck in pairs are rotating by keeping the point of contact fixed. This ensures that they occupy all possible orientations. For a third molecule the volume unavailable is the volume of the large sphere with radius 2 r where $r$ is the radius of each molecule. Therefore the volume unavailable is $\frac{4}{3}\pi {{(2r)}^{3}}=8\times \frac{4}{3}\pi {{r}^{3}}$

This volume is unavailable due to two molecules. Thus, due to one molecule the volume unavailable is 4 times its volume.

$\therefore \quad b=4\times \frac{4}{3}\pi {{r}^{3}}\times 6.923\times {{10}^{28}}$

Thus Van der Walls equation for 1 mole of real gas is given as $\left( \text{P}+\frac{\text{a}}{{{\text{V}}^{2}}} \right)(\text{V}-\text{b})=\text{RT}$

Significance of a and b

The constant ‘a’ is a measure of Van der Walls forces of attraction existing between the molecules of a given gas. The greater the value of a, the greater is the strength of the Van der Waals forces. The greater the value of a, the greater is the ease with which a gas can be liquefied.

‘$b$’ is just the incompressible volume per mole of any gas.

If temperature is high and pressure is low, the Van der Waals constants a and become insignificant and the Vander Waals equation becomes ideal gas equation.

concepts.title: Vander Waal's Equation
concepts.conceptBody:
Vander Waal's Equation

In 1873, Van der Waals modified the ideal gas equation by suggesting that the gas molecules were not point masses but behave like rigid spheres having a certain diameter and that there exist intermolecular forces of attraction between them. The two correction terms introduced by Van der Waals has been described before.

The Vander Waals equation for 1 mole of a real gas is

$\left( \text{P}+\frac{\text{a}}{\text{V}_{\text{m}}^{2}} \right)\left( {{\text{V}}_{\text{m}}}-\text{b} \right)=\text{RT}$

The equation can be rewritten as $P{{V}_{m}}-Pb+\frac{a}{{{V}_{m}}}-\frac{ab}{V_{m}^{2}}=RT$

$\text{or }\quad \text{P}{{\text{V}}_{\text{m}}}=\text{RT}+\text{Pb}-\frac{\text{a}}{{{\text{V}}_{\text{m}}}}+\frac{\text{ab}}{\text{V}_{\text{m}}^{2}}$

Dividing Eq. by RT

$\frac{P{{V}_{m}}}{RT}=\frac{RT}{RT}+\frac{Pb}{RT}-\frac{a}{{{V}_{m}}RT}+\frac{ab}{V_{m}^{2}RT}$

$\text{Z}=1+\frac{\text{Pb}}{\text{RT}}-\frac{\text{a}}{{{\text{V}}_{\text{m}}}\text{RT}}+\frac{\text{ab}}{\text{V}_{\text{m}}^{2}\text{RT}}$

For ${{\text{H}}_{2}}$ gas, the volume of a is extremely small (as it is very difficult to liquefy) so, all terms involving ‘ $a$ ‘ in the numerator of equation can be reduced to

$\text{Z}=1+\frac{\text{Pb}}{\text{RT}}$

It clearly indicates that with the increase of pressure, $\text{Z}$ increases and is always greater than unity.

For methane, and $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$, in the low pressure region, ${{\text{V}}_{\text{m}}}$ would be very large and in comparison to ${{\text{V}}_{\text{m}}}$, ‘b’ cannot be ignored. The equation can be rewritten as

$\text{Z}=1+\frac{\text{Pb}}{\text{RT}}+\left[ \frac{\text{a}}{{{\text{V}}_{\text{m}}}\text{RT}}\left( \frac{\text{b}}{{{\text{V}}_{\text{m}}}}-1 \right) \right]$

Since $b<<{{V}_{m}}\text{, so }\frac{b}{{{V}_{m}}}$ would be extremely smaller than 1. Thus, the term $\left[ \frac{a}{{{V}_{m}}RT}\left( \frac{b}{{{V}_{m}}}-1 \right) \right]$would be negative and greater in magnitude than positive term $\frac{\text{Pb}}{\text{RT}}$. Consequently, the $\text{Z}$ is less than unity.

With the increase of pressure in the low pressure region, both the terms, $\frac{\text{Pb}}{\text{RT}}$ and $\left[ \frac{a}{{{V}_{m}}RT}\left( \frac{b}{{{V}_{m}}}-1 \right) \right]$ increases but the increase in negative term, $\left[ \frac{a}{{{V}_{m}}RT}\left( \frac{b}{{{V}_{m}}}-1 \right) \right]$ would be more than the increase in positive term, $\left( \frac{\text{Pb}}{\text{RT}} \right)$, thus $\text{Z}$ decreases continuously. This means that the difference in magnitude of negative term and positive term continuously increases. When this difference becomes maximum, the minima are obtained. Thereafter, the increase in negative term is lower than the increase in positive term. Thus, Z starts increasing but is still less than unity. Then, a time comes when the magnitude of negative terms and positive terms become equal. Under such conditions, $\text{Z}$ becomes equal to unity and when the magnitude of positive term becomes more than the negative term, the value of $\text{Z}$ becomes greater than unity.

The nature of the curve is like this because in the low pressure region the effect of attractive forces (‘$a$’ factor) dominates while in the high pressure region, the size effect (‘$b$’ factor) will dominate the behaviour of the gas.

concepts.title: Vander waals equation at high and low pressure
concepts.conceptBody:
Vander waals equation at high and low pressure

Note:

(a) At very low pressure for 1 mole gas ${{\text{P}}^{\prime }}$ and $\text{nb}$ can be neglected.

$\text{PV}=\text{RT}$

(b) At moderate pressure, nb can be neglected

$\left( \text{P}+\frac{\text{a}}{{{\text{V}}^{2}}} \right)(\text{V})=\text{RT}$

$\text{Z}=\frac{\text{PV}}{\text{RT}}\text{ so Z}=1-\frac{\text{a}}{\text{RTV}}$

$\text{Z}<1$

(c) At high pressure

${{\text{P}}^{\prime }}$ can be ignored but $\text{b}$ cannot be ignored.

So, $P(V-b)=RT$

$\text{Z}=\frac{\text{PV}}{\text{RT}}=1+\frac{\text{Pb}}{\text{RT}}$

$\text{Z}>1$

In case of $\text{H}$ and $\text{He}$ due to their small size, we always ignore the internal pressure $\left( {{\text{P}}^{\prime }} \right.$ or $\left. \frac{\text{a}{{\text{n}}^{2}}}{~{{\text{V}}^{2}}} \right)$. So, they always show $\text{Z}>1$ at moderate and high pressures.
